['xprop', '-root', '\t$0', '_NET_ACTIVE_WINDOW']

IMHO, shlex has the following advantages:
* It is more readable: less visual clutter (quotes and commas), especially if you have a long command.
* It is more easily testable: just copy paste the command and run it on your shell.

All in all I believe that using shlex is a better practice in general and I use it on my own code, but this is not my repo, so if you prefer a list, a list it shall be :)

@peplin I did verify this using real hardware that returned data, so the data I built and tested the proposed changes on came from `gatttool`. The problem was that I got a `bytes` string in Python 3 (the binary text type there), whereas in Python 2 I got a `str` (the binary text type there). The `bytesarray.fromhex` method accepted only `str` input in both environments, so I added a `decode` to make the `bytes` into a `str` in Python 3.
I believe that the `pexpect`/`gatttool` solution is consistent in the sense that it returns binary representations of string data in both environments, the only problem is that Python 3 is less adapted to use such representation in general.

It wouyld be better to use  ```flask_restful.unpack``` (will be merged soon) to properly unpack response above:
```python
elif e.__class__ in self._error_handlers:
    handler = self._error_handlers[e.__class__]
    result = handler(e)
    default_data, code, headers = restful.unpack(result)
elif self._default_error_handler:
    result = self._default_error_handler(e)
    default_data, code, headers = restful.unpack(result)
```
The only problem is that `unpack` always take 200 as default status code.
If you wait a little bit, I'l merge `unpack` allowing to override the default status code and to write:
```python
if isinstance(e, HTTPException):
    code = e.code
    default_data = {
        'message': getattr(e, 'description', HTTP_STATUS_CODES.get(code, ''))
     }
     headers = e.get_response().headers
elif e.__class__ in self._error_handlers:
    handler = self._error_handlers[e.__class__]
    result = handler(e)
    default_data, code, headers = restful.unpack(result, 500)
elif self._default_error_handler:
    result = self._default_error_handler(e)
    default_data, code, headers = restful.unpack(result, 500)
else:
    code = 500
    default_data = {
        'message': HTTP_STATUS_CODES.get(code, str(e)),
    }
    headers = Headers()
```

Just realized that this block of code needs to be updated to handle the fact that we no longer have a jQuery element to add the class to. You'll need to add the classes applied in `setSelectJobStyles` to the interpolated job element, then re-select the job once it exists. The first part should be relatively straightforward, the second might be tricky -- probably the right way of doing this is to make the resultset store model no longer keep a reference to the job element -- we should get that element ourselves where needed based purely on the id. 

If you want to try tackling this, you'll need to modify `ui/js/models/resultsets_store.js`, in particular the getSelectedJob and setSelectedJob to only keep a reference to the selected job's id, not el. You'll also need to add a jQuery selector inside clonejobs.js to get the element dynamically based on the id (where we were getting it via getSelectedJob before).

You're right Stu. We don't want to ever use this idiom because isort changes the order where it no longer works as expected. `standard_library.install_aliases()` is supposed to happen *before* the relevant import, but isort always moves it below every import.

Instead, we have to use `from future.moves import x`. The tricky part is figuring out what `standard_library.install_aliases()` was overriding. I use autocomplete and try out every import to see where it's coming from, e.g. see if `future.moves.contextlib` exists. Sometimes I can't find anything, so I delete the import because I think it might be an over-optimization?

```python
import threading
import six
import sys


def a():
  return b()
def b():
  return c()
def c():
  raise ValueError('Oh, dear!')

def with_explicit_raise():
  try:
    a()
  except ValueError as e:
    raise ValueError(e.message + ' There was a problem.')


def with_six_reraise():
  try:
    a()
  except ValueError as e:
    (unused_type, unused_value, traceback) = sys.exc_info()
    six.reraise(
        ValueError,
        ValueError(e.message + ' There was a problem.'),
        traceback)


if __name__ == '__main__':

```
